channel.title: 7. Parallelism on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in 7. Parallelism on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 7.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Parallelism is all around us, and it lends clarity and forcefulness to writing. “Early to bed, early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise.” In the above example, the three adjectives “healthy,” “wealthy,” and “wise” are parallel. “Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears; I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.” In this second example, a parallel structure is made up of two verbs in infinitive form (“to + verb”).

item.title: 7.2 There are Common Triggers that Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content People often wonder when to check for parallelism. 💡MUST KNOW: There are firmly established triggers that alert us to check for proper parallelism. Each time we encounter one of these triggers in a sentence, we check to be sure that the elements in that sentence are parallel. In this chapter, we’ll cover each of these parallelism triggers, such as the FANBOYS conjunctions, correlative conjunctions, including “not only … but also,” and items found in a list.

item.title: 7.3 Parallelism Has Little to Do with How a Sentence Sounds to Your Ear
item.description: Lesson Content A common theme we’ll encounter is that parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds. We’ll encounter many sentences that “sound” parallel, yet are not. 💡MUST KNOW: Parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds. Instead, parallelism has everything to do with how a sentence is structured. Don’t rely on your “ear”! Let’s now discuss a concept that is foundational in understanding parallelism: structures built around coordinating conjunctions, also known as FANBOYS, must be parallel.

item.title: 7.4 FANBOYS Always Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content We already know that the coordinating conjunctions are “for,” “and,” “nor,” “but,” “or,” “yet,” and “so.” We use the mnemonic device FANBOYS to remember them. 💡MUST KNOW: The coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. These FANBOYS conjunctions are always parallelism markers, and a structure built around one of the FANBOYS must be parallel. In other words, any time we encounter one of these FANBOYS conjunctions, we must be vigilant in ensuring that parallelism is properly maintained in the sentence in which it’s found.

item.title: 7.4.1 How to Determine Whether a Structure Built Around a FANBOYS Is Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content There are many methods that can be used to determine whether structures in a sentence are written with proper parallelism, but many of these are either just too grammatical or too complicated to work well on test day. Instead, we’re going to use a method that is based on logic and is easy to use on test day. To determine whether a structure built around a FANBOYS is parallel:

item.title: 7.5 Structures Built Around FANBOYS are Essentially Lists of Two Items
item.description: Lesson Content Without realizing it, you’ve been not only learning about parallelism in structures built across FANBOYS but also learning about parallelism in lists, which frequently appear in SC questions. More specifically, you’ve been learning about parallelism in lists of two items because the structures built across FANBOYS that we have been discussing are essentially two-item lists. 💡MUST KNOW: Structures built around FANBOYS conjunctions are two-element lists. These lists must maintain proper parallel structure.

item.title: 7.6 Parallelism in Lists
item.description: Lesson Content A list consists of two or more items in a series, and the items in a list are generally separated by commas or coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS). The items in a list must be parallel. 💡MUST KNOW: A list consists of two or more items in a series, often separated by commas. The items in a list must be parallel. The good news is that, because you already know a lot about checking for parallelism across the FANBOYS conjunctions, the skills needed for checking for parallelism in a list will be much easier to master.

item.title: 7.6.1 Carry Over in Lists
item.description: Lesson Content “Carrying over” simply refers to the concept that the sentence structures that introduce a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten. 💡MUST KNOW: “Carrying over” refers to the concept that certain elements of a structure involving a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten. You saw this concept (although we didn’t use the term carrying over) a short while ago in the sections on parallelism across FANBOYS.

item.title: 7.6.2 How to Determine Whether the Items in a List are Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content To determine whether the items in a list are parallel, we’ll follow the following steps: Step1: Notice the list in the sentence. Lists are always parallelism triggers. Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called. Step 2: Identify the elements of the list. Our next move in analyzing a sentence for proper parallelism in a list is to identify the elements of the list.

item.title: 7.6.3 Repeating Elements Essential to What Introduces a List Cannot Be Haphazardly Placed in a List
item.description: Lesson Content From our discussion of carrying over, we know that we need not explicitly write a word such as “to” before every element in a list in a sentence like the following: The members of the committee decided to reduce marketing expenditures, to increase headcount significantly, and to change the schedule. In other words, one “to” in front of the first element of the list is perfect, as you’ve seen in sentences like the following:

item.title: 7.6.4 Sometimes a Word in a List Must Be Repeated For Clarity
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to be clear, it’s necessary to repeat certain words by placing them before every item in the list. Let’s start with prepositions.

item.title: 7.6.5 Sometimes Prepositions Must be Repeated for a List to Clearly Convey a Logical Meaning
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a preposition, such as “for,” “of,” “to,” “in,” “by,” or “because of” by placing the preposition before every item in the list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a preposition, such as “for,” “of,” “to,” “in,” “by,” or “because of” by placing the preposition before every item in the list.

item.title: 7.6.6 Sometimes “That” Must be Repeated to Make a List Clear
item.description: Lesson Content Similar to what we saw with repeated prepositions, sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a “that” by placing it before every item in the list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a “that” by placing it before every item in the list.

item.title: 7.6.7 Nested Lists
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes we’ll encounter a list within a list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes we’ll encounter a list within a list. We call these structures nested lists. In other words, a nested list is a list within one of the elements of another list. Consider the following example sentence: Please leave your name and your number and explain why you called. Notice that we have a two-part list. Please leave (your name and your number) and explain (why you called).

item.title: 7.6.8 Understanding the Carry Over Within a Nested List
item.description: Lesson Content We now know how to recognize a nested list, and we understand the importance of keeping the outer list and the nested list straight. Our next important concept to understand is what carries over in the nested list and in the outer list. Please leave your name and your number and explain why you called. We know that the outer list is the following. Please: leave (your name and your number)

item.title: 7.6.9 Correct Answers that Fix a Broken List by Changing It to a Nested List
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, it’s common to encounter SC questions in which the original sentence contains a broken list (a list constructed without proper parallelism). 💡MUST KNOW: It’s common to encounter SC questions in which the original sentence contains a broken list (a list constructed without proper parallelism). In these situations, it’s common to find that the correct answer fixes the broken list by changing it into a nested list.

item.title: 7.7 Correlative Conjunctions Must be Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content In Sentence Correction questions, we commonly encounter correlative conjunctions, the most important of which to learn are: not only … but also not only … but … as well not … but both … and either … or neither … nor 💡MUST KNOW: The most important correlative conjunctions to learn are “not only … but also,” “not only … but … as well,” “not … but,” “both … and,” “either … or,” “neither … nor.

item.title: 7.7.1 The Correlative Conjunction “Not Only … But Also”
item.description: Lesson Content One of the most commonly used correlative conjunctions is “not only … but also.” Likely, you’re used to seeing this conjunction in sentences. For example: Marty eats not only avocados but also bananas. Jeff not only visited Patagonia but also spent time on Easter Island. She wrote him not only a love letter but also a poem. Not only does Inder love studying for the GMAT, but also he loves writing his essays.

item.title: 7.7.2 Whatever Precedes the First Part of a “Not Only … But Also” Structure Carries Over to the Second Part.
item.description: Lesson Content Whatever precedes the first part of a “not only … but also” structure is understood to carry over to the second part of the structure, and thus does not have to be repeated in order for a complete, logical thought to be expressed. 💡MUST KNOW: Whatever precedes the first part of a “not only … but also” correlative structure is understood to carry over to, without being written in, the second part of the correlative structure.

item.title: 7.7.3 Whatever Appears After “Not Only” Does Not Carry Over to After “But Also.”
item.description: Lesson Content In the last section, we saw that an essential element that precedes “not only” is understood to carry over, without being written, to what comes after “but also.” In contrast, an element that comes directly after “not only” does not carry over to after “but also.” So, if any such element is necessary after “but also,” it must be repeated. 💡MUST KNOW: In a “not only … but also” structure, elements that follow “not only” are not understood to carry over to after “but also.

item.title: 7.7.4 The Placement of Words Between “But” and “Also” Can Result in an Illogical Sentence.
item.description: Lesson Content When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism. 💡MUST KNOW: When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.

item.title: 7.7.5 Other Correlative Conjunctions
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, you’ve already learned a great deal about the correlative structure “not only … but also.” The good news, as promised, is that the patterns that apply to “not only … but also” apply almost identically to the other correlative conjunctions. Let’s continue by talking about carry over and correlative conjunctions.

item.title: 7.7.6 Carry-Over and other Correlative Conjunctions
item.description: Lesson Content As you saw with the correlative conjunction “not only … but also,” whatever essential element precedes “not only” is understood to carry over to the “but also” part of the construction. not only … but … as well not … but both … and either … or neither … nor For example, consider the following sentence: Incorrect: Each day, Sam is charged with either mopping the floors or he must take out the trash.

item.title: 7.8 Other Structures that Demand Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content So far in this chapter, we’ve seen a number of situations in which parallelism must be maintained. X rather than Y mistake X for Y prefer X to Y range from X to Y more X than Y (in certain contexts) Consider the following example sentence: The team traveled by rail rather than flew to the tournament. Notice, what precedes “rather than,” “traveled,” is parallel to what follows “rather than,” “flew.

item.title: 7.9 Parallel Does Not Always Mean Exactly the Same.
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes in learning about parallelism, people come away with the idea that the elements of a parallel structure must always match each other closely. Let’s review some common situations in which elements that may not seem parallel are considered parallel, starting with the fact that verbs in different tenses can be perfectly parallel.

item.title: 7.9.1 Verbs That Are in Different Tenses Can be Perfectly Parallel.
item.description: Lesson Content Some people believe that verbs that are in different tenses are not parallel to each other. 💡MUST KNOW: Verbs that are in different tenses can be parallel. For example, consider the following sentence that has different tenses, past perfect and past continuous, in the same list: The ecologist said that genetically modified fish had found their way into the lake, had grown quickly, and were eating the other fish.

item.title: 7.9.2 Active Voice and Passive Voice in the Same List Can be Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content One might assume that it’s incorrect to have both active voice verbs and passive voice verbs in the same list. 💡MUST KNOW: There is nothing wrong with having active voice verbs and passive voice verbs together in a list. Consider the following example: Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and was accepted. As we already know well, each component of a list must work logically with the essential element or elements that introduce the list.

item.title: 7.9.3 Clauses in a Sentence Do Not Have to Be Structured in the Same Way to Be Parallel.
item.description: Lesson Content When considering parallelism of clauses in a sentence, there is one simple rule that we can follow: Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel. After all, clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs. 💡MUST KNOW: Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel because clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.

item.title: 7.10 When, other than in degree of parallelism, two versions of a sentence seem equally correct, choose the more parallel version.
item.description: Lesson Content We have discussed the fact that parallelism is largely based on logic and the fact that structures don’t have to match exactly in order to be acceptably parallel. At the same time, we have seen that some structures are more parallel than others. Rome is one of the few cities in the world where a great number of well-preserved ancient structures can be found and that has many examples of stunning Renaissance architecture.

item.title: 7.11 Gerunds and Infinitives – Special Cases
item.description: Lesson Content Gerunds, verb forms that end in –ing, such as “sailing” or “writing,” are essentially nouns, and infinitives, verbs preceded by “to,” such as “to sail” or “to write,” can also be used as nouns. However, despite this similarity between gerunds and infinitives, the convention followed by the GMAT is that gerunds and infinitives are not parallel. 💡MUST KNOW: The GMAT prefers versions that do not use gerunds and infinitives together in lists.
